paragraphs.text: A GREAT deal has been written about
for nearly a quarter of a century, but some of the facts seem to have escaped notice.
I shall write them down here as briefly as possible for the use of the coming
, although an Indian, was almost white and of extraordinary intelligence and sensibility.
, who attended her in her last illness, told me that he thought perhaps she was not of Indian extraction at all. But to get on with the facts. When
they began to gather in all the docile or friendly Indians and utilize as well as civilize them. After some years they found out that
lying off about twenty miles from their harbor of
the most intelligent and gentle people
there that had yet been discovered on the continent. They were loyal, affectionate, and loved their
in the sea above all things.
. They had no wood to build boats with, and so
the long black and barren mountain of land
away to the south was as inaccessible to them as is the moon to us.

They had queer little houses of stone on the one low cliff, and they were very skillful in spearing fish and capturing all sorts of sea birds. They made mocassins [sic] for their feet out of sealskins, for seals were and are to this day so abundant there that you can scarcely walk about without treading on some of the little baby seals. And all along to right and left, as you pass by, you see the mild-eyed mother seal nursing her baby at her breast, as she half rests and reclines against a rock, and lifts her kind, human eyes to yours, as if to ask you please not hurt her little hairy baby.
I found the eggs of water fowl so plentiful when I visited
almost twenty years ago to gather material for an English magazine that it was absolutely impossible to walk about without crushing great numbers under foot.
dressed their bodies not in furs, but in feathers. They skinned the sea lions and out of the skins they made remarkably beautiful garments of all hues and fashions.
And this wonderful skill in all their simple walks of life, together with their perfect docility—for it seems they knew nothing at all about war or even an[g]er—tempted
to undertake the transportation of the whole tribe, numbering less than two hundred, to their settlement at

One was to the effect that she had hidden away
in the rocks on consenting to go on board

, so as to make sure that it at least should not be torn from its happy little home in the sea and made to serve the strangers, and then finding she was being carried away to return no more, she, in her desperation, dashed through her guards and so got away. A kindlier story is to the effect that
, but that she did not know it and thought it had been forgotten. Still another version of the story runs to the effect that

was about to be devoured by numbers of dogs that were left behind, and that she leaped into the sea to save it. But this is the most unkindly and the hardest of all the versions of this extraordinary story. For to believe this last is to believe that
was indeed destroyed by the dogs; since
when found there and taken to
nearly a quarter of a century ago was found entirely alone.
My own notion of the matter is that
entrusted the capture and transportation of

entirely to some cruel leader of their few seamen and soldiers; that with the usual high handed audacity of the early Spaniards he decoyed them all on board, and only this one could get away. I see no reason for believing there was any baby in the case at all.
And now years and years rolled by. A beautiful little city blossomed out at

. A huge stone cathedral, still standing there with its frieze of human skulls set in stonelike cement, was reared by the hands of the converts and the captives; but she saw nothing, knew nothing of this. For her there was no change day after day, year after year. The same rise and fall of the sea, the same solemn march of the seasons, the same silent marching and countermarching of the stars above her and about her on the edge of the sea, the same endless, awful monotony of the rising and the going down of the sun and the moon and the stars. And that was all. It seems incredible to think that any set of human beings could have been so entirely brutal as to leave
there all these years so utterly and so desolately alone.
She did not even have the goats of Robinson Crusoe, to say nothing of
She had only the dogs. And they became so numerous and so wild, as we shall presently see, as to be the greatest terrors.
But it should here be explained as a sort of justification of

that they had hardily got their religious settlement solidly founded when they were recalle[d] by the King of Spain, or at least made powerless by the confiscation of nearly all their privileges and their property. And so other people took their places, and time swept on.
After years and years gold was found in

and ships began to sail by in great numbers. One ship after another reported seeing a lone figure like that of a human being standing on a little pyramid of heaped up stones and looking out after the ship till lost to sight.
who had been left for so many years on
and the circumstances as I have set them down, so far as the facts were known at

But everybody was busy getting gold. We had no lighthouse or government stations then as now. Our acquisition of the vast seashore out here was new. We had not yet explored or set foot on the half of our possessions. And so time still swept on and
was still left with her wild dogs in
And now one of the great steamers carrying gold and passengers,
not infrequently now. And the story went about that the little pyramid of rocks was gradually growing higher and
that mounted it taller and taller as time went by.
Finally the American spirit could endure this idea of lone and endless captivity no longer.
and some others from I know not where hired
and sailed straight for the relief of
. She stood high and lone on the heap of stones and waited for their coming.

And what could have been her thoughts? Would they, too, pass on and away to the left and to the right as a thousand other ships had passed? Would they come to her? Could they come? And what if they should come? Would they devour her; [sic] as the wild dogs that gathered about her were waiting to devour her? Landing without trouble, for the ocean here is still and restful as a rule, they walked away over the sea birds' eggs and through the swarms of seal straight up toward
on the high heap of stones. It did not attempt to run away.

And here is the grimmest and most pathetic part of it all; she could not run away without danger of being devoured by the horde of wild dogs that literally sat in a circle around her, waiting, watching for her to come down.

She had been years building her little fortress, and she had built it with all the care and skill of a trained engineer. Here was a stone house for her provisions; there a cell for water, and here one worn and crooked pass where she could possibly beat back the dogs with her club as long as she had strength. But when strength failed her? She had not been unmindful of failing strength in the end, however, and so had built a little tower on top, where she could at the last lie down and die.
told me that she surely could have held out but a little time longer.

Strange as it may seem, she laughed at the pity offered her. She would not come down to them, but when they came up into her little retreat she laughed outright and hid her face in her rich robe of feathers.
In fact, as this same man told me, she laughed almost continually, perhaps to the poor creature's only form of expression.
For, of course, she could not understand one word that any one spoke to her.
And one of the interesting things about it all is when they got her to
was left alive to talk with her.
, who was then the principal citizen in
is still there to verify all that has been said, told me that he took from
with her and that she began to laugh as soon as he took hold of her hand to help her ashore. And he further told me that as he walked up
a man in the street on horseback
, and that on the sight of
she sat right flat down in the dust and laughed and laughed as if she could never stop laughing.
Of course the strange story and the news of the rescuing from
soon spread all over the world.
home to secure her for his "greatest show on earth," and the
promised to make ample provision for her comfort and well being at home.
had fairly ceased to laugh at the sight of the first horse she had ever seen she felt ill.
All was done by the good people of

She had the best of care and certainly the best of physicians attended her. But she rapidly declined and one day while sitting on the grass eating a piece of watermelon she suddenly turned her face away to
, looked a moment, and then sank down on the grass, dead!

They found a great many fishhooks hidden away in her new clothes and also a number of needles, for fishhooks and needles were the wonder and the delight of her heart, and this gave rise to the idea that was getting ready to swim back to
assured me that she was perfectly content in

and had no notion of running away. He said, with reason, that it was but natural that she should, after her long enforced habits of hoarding of things, hide away fishhooks and needles, as the two things were important to her in her pitifully limited vision of life.
She left many things of very cunning workmanship still to be seen in
. But her gorgeous robe of feathers, made entirely from the few green feathers found on the heads of certain ducks and composed of more than one thousand pieces was sent to
and is to be seen in

She caught these ducks with fishhooks made of bone and sinew. She sewed her robe of ever so many pieces together with sinews taken from the backs of seal, her only needle being a sharpened bone. No wonder that all the wealth of
, to her, was not worth so much as a few fishhooks and needles.
